replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text: Accents don't have anything to do with the inflections and tones that tell you whether a person is happy or angry. Even if it's a totally foreign language you can tell the emotion of what they are saying, that's mostly in the nonverbal cues. A guy who sounds his words a little differently doesn't mean he doesn't sound angry when he's angry. You can tell when he's asking a question just like you can anyone else, right? Or when he's being ironic or sarcastic? It's not even a different language. The only thing an accent should effect is that you might not actually recognize some of the words he uses because they aren't spoken the way you are familiar with or you never heard them before. Everything else about how he speaks and communicates is exactly like everyone else who speaks English.

text: Just come out of watching this and still processing it but tonally I think it's what we thought we were gunna get with the last movie... and I say that as someone who liked 28 Years Later overall. I get the comparison with Boyle's style but the change in director is noticeable and necessary especially for this chapter. It's stripped back, grubby, uncompromising, and treats the Jimmies dead seriously (rather than for lolz in the last moments of 28YL) which makes them so much more sinister, like Clockwork Orange types. Erin Kellyman didn't bother me but that's only cuz I never saw Falcon and Winter Soldier and her character is one of the only in the film that is protective of Spike/stands up to Jimmy so by that alone I warmed to her. I echo everything said about O'Connell, and Fiennes is terrific. His resolution is kinda bittersweet but he goes out in a blaze of glory and doing the honourable thing, surrounded by friends and knowing what he acheived with Samson.

replies.author: @J1mston
replies.text: I took the Savile stuff as an extension of the introspection of the English psyche that the first movie was going for. To put it bluntly, we English and British more broadly have a bit of a habit of dressing up as some of the worst people in history as a form of mockery towards them. Osama bin Laden costumes, along with that kind of terrorist get up was a popular fancy dress at one point. Type “Jimmy Savile” into Google and it wants to finish it off by adding costume. One of our princes even infamously had a bash at inappropriate fancy dress. It’s a part of who we are as a people so adding it into a film that explores who we are seemed appropriate.

replies.text:
In the days before the internet, critics and reviewers (of books, films, music, art, architecture, whatever) had usually built a shaky career on years of working under strict editorial control. You couldn't get away with spouting any old sh¹te because your boss would say, "You SURE 'bout that?", put a thick blue line through your masterpiece, and tell you to f*-k off and do it again, properly, because no bl**dy hack was getting space in HIS prestige publication (even if you worked for a tiny local newspaper).

You either learned objectivity, or you didn't get published or broadcast.

With the internet, such decent critics were buried under a wave of talentless, undisciplined, untrained, ignorant influencers and 'opinionators', who had 100x more power to reach an audience than  old-school critics, but absolutely no standards or integrity, no objectivity, and no ability to self-censor.

It's rare to find a modern critic worthy of the name. BELIEVE THAT-T-T! 😁

author: @CelticClobber
text:
People say the two are disjointed but I prefer to look at them like they clash on purpose with great contrast. Ying and yang

The first part being a lot more action orientated and showing the price of survival in such a dangerous world with the relentless infected that can't be reasoned or bargained with. Pure survival at all costs and the coming of age of a boy forced to be a man far too early in his life.

The second movie really emphasizing the evil that man is capable of but it surprisingly also made me empathize with Jimmy, a 8 year old boy who lost his parents in the face of a zombie apocalypse and had to endure psychosis / schizophrenia without any love, support or adequate mental health support or treatment.

The empathy and compassion shown to the Alpha, who was just a little boy on a train without a ticket 28 years beforehand.
The conflict and turmoil in Samson's mind as the anti psychotic drug gave him a sense of clarity, to the horrors of what he has become and how he has lived his life under the vice grip of the rage virus.
Cillian's cameo at the end showing that even after all of the shit he's been through and how hard he's worked to prosper and carve out a meaningful life for his daughter to grow up in the apocalypse, he risks to lose it all because of the empathy he shows the two fleeing surviors.

I don't know if I'm seeing things that aren't there but its my take on it.

How's that? >:)

replies.author: @SpaceMeshYT
replies.text:
HOWZAAATTT

As for TBT, it felt like a lot of things from 28 Years have been pushed forward. Some questions answered, some new questions come up. It's more payoff from the previous film, but still giving us more to look forward to.

author: @DaleTomlinson-w6c
text:
I thought it was absolutely excellent. Incredible dialogue, masterful performances for Dr Kelson and Jimmy Crystal, and a really tight and interesting story which leaves you with more new questions than answers - but in a good way. I liked that the entire movie is over the course of a few days or so - building the obvious tension between the good of humanity (Kelson) curing this thing and the bad of humanity (Jimmy) keeping it going. The first meeting of those two characters was my favourite dialogue I have seen in the cinema for a decade - at least in this genre. I also thought the Samson storyline was incredibly unique and I loved seeing that process. 

It feels like it was originally supposed to be one movie about how Spike left home with his terminally ill mum, gets taken in by a group he thought were going to be superheroes and then accidentally takes them on a destruction course heading directly for home - but that they had to change things around as it was too long and complex for a single movie. I actually really enjoyed Years, but I think Years is actually better now having seen the conclusion in The Bone Temple than it was having seen it as a single movie - and not just because it clears up the whole situation with the Jimmy Savile Power Rangers!

replies.text: @brunomonteiro7788 The big guy benefitted from the love Doc showed him, as well. The big guy stopped being triggered by the Doc after the transfusion.
